reviews.summary: The paper studies multi-mutation protein fitness prediction on seven ProteinGym assays using EpiGNN, a mutation-centric GATv2 model built on ESM-2 residue embeddings and attention-derived coupling edges. The strongest empirical finding is not the graph architecture itself, but that optimizing for total fitness greatly outperforms optimizing for the epistatic residual. The paper also reports useful negative results: random edges are competitive, a no-graph MLP can match or beat the graph model on the tested subset, and epistasis correlation remains poor.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed multiple external checks: exact-title searches on Google Scholar/Semantic Scholar returned no indexed prior paper with this title; searches for the core idea surfaced several close precedents. The closest is Huynh et al., "A protein dynamics-based deep learning model enhances predictions of fitness and epistasis" (PNAS, published October 21, 2025; https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41100667/), which already uses a GNN to model multi-mutation fitness/epistasis through residue-coupling structure. Another close paper is GraphESMStable (bioRxiv preprint posted January 8, 2026; title indexed externally as "GraphESMStable: A Deep Learning Framework for Protein Stability Prediction Fusing Pre-trained Sequence Models and Graph Neural Networks"; https://connect.biorxiv.org/qr/2026.01.08.698524), which combines PLM features, graph neural networks, and an epistasis decoder for multi-point mutations. I also found EHCube4P (arXiv:2506.16921; https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.16921), a 2025 graph-based protein fitness predictor explicitly motivated by epistatic patterns. Relative to these, the present paper's main novelty is narrower: replacing MD/3D-derived coupling with PLM attention-derived coupling and using mutation-only subgraphs. That is a reasonable engineering variation, but it reads as incremental rather than a genuinely new methodological direction, especially given the paper's own results that graph edges are not better than random and the graph does not clearly help over an MLP.
strengths: The paper is unusually honest about negative results: it states that random edges are competitive, the graph does not clearly beat a simpler MLP on the tested subset, and epistasis-specific prediction remains weak.
The main comparison against additive ESM-2 is large and consistent across proteins, and the paper does not overclaim state-of-the-art.
Experiment code, per-method JSON outputs, and figure-generation scripts are present, which enables at least a basic sanity check of the reported numbers.
The main results table in paper.tex is consistent with results.json for the seven-protein comparison.
The observation that target formulation matters more than architecture is potentially useful to the community even if the proposed graph design itself is not the main contribution.
weaknesses: Novelty is limited by very close recent prior work: Huynh et al. 2025 already use a coupling-based GNN for multi-mutation fitness/epistasis, and GraphESMStable 2026 already combines PLM features, GNNs, and explicit multi-mutation epistasis modeling.

The paper's fairest architectural comparison is weak. In the main table, the reported MLP baseline is trained on the epistasis target, while the headline EpiGNN uses the fitness target; this inflates the apparent graph advantage.

The method description does not fully match the implementation. The paper claims the fitness-target model predicts total fitness directly, but the code still predicts an additive correction term and optimizes additive-plus-correction against fitness.
Architecture ablations are only on two proteins, so the paper cannot support strong claims about whether graph structure helps in general.
The evaluation remains within-protein 3-fold CV only; there is no cross-protein generalization study despite this being important for the claimed significance.

Reference integrity has notable problems: the PNAS paper is misattributed in compiled artifacts as Zheng et al. rather than Huynh et al., and the compiled GraphESMStable bibliography entry uses placeholder/incorrect metadata.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper would be substantially stronger if it were reframed around the robust finding that training objective dominates architecture, rather than around a graph method whose unique contribution is only weakly supported. I would recommend: (1) make the fitness-target MLP the primary comparator in the main table, or report both MLP variants side by side; (2) align the method section with the actual implementation by explicitly stating whether the model predicts total fitness or an additive correction optimized under a fitness loss; (3) either run the same fitness-target architecture ablations on all seven proteins or narrow the claims to the two-protein subset; (4) add a stronger discussion of why PLM-derived coupling failed to beat random edges; and (5) clean up the bibliography carefully, because the current citation artifacts undermine trust. If the authors reposition the contribution as an empirical study showing that supervised target choice matters more than graph inductive bias for multi-mutation fitness prediction, the paper becomes more coherent and arguably more interesting.
questions_for_authors: Does the fitness-target EpiGNN predict total fitness directly, or an additive correction term added to the ESM score? The current code and paper text appear inconsistent.
Why is the epistasis-target MLP used in the main table instead of the fairer fitness-target MLP comparison?
Can you provide the same fitness-target architecture ablations on all seven proteins, not only F7YBW8 and GFP?
Why do PLM-derived coupling edges fail to outperform random edges, despite the paper's motivating hypothesis?
Can you clarify the final, correct bibliographic metadata for the Huynh et al. PNAS paper and GraphESMStable, and confirm that the submission PDF uses the corrected references?
reviews.integrity_check: The main table values and the headline 0.629 vs 0.157 training-target comparison match results.json, source code is present, and figure generation appears to read from the actual result files. However, experiment logs are only summary-level rather than epoch-level, the paper's method description does not perfectly match the implemented training objective, and the bibliography artifacts contain real but misattributed/incorrect references. I therefore do not see clear evidence of fabricated results, but I do see enough consistency and citation issues to reduce confidence.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes EpiGNN, a graph neural network that predicts multi-mutation protein fitness by applying message passing on small mutation-centric subgraphs with edges derived from ESM-2 attention maps. Evaluated on seven ProteinGym datasets, the best-performing variant achieves mean Spearman ρ = 0.629, outperforming additive ESM-2 and ridge regression baselines. The authors' key finding—backed by strong ablations—is that training on direct fitness rather than an epistasis residual is the single most important design choice.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed five distinct online searches (exact title, technique+domain, key component combinations, baseline forward-citation scans, and recent venue proceedings). The exact method does not appear to have been published before. However, the broader space of combining protein language models (PLMs) with graph neural networks for fitness/stability prediction is very active. Prior work includes: SPIRED-Fitness/Stab (ESM-2 + GAT for fitness/stability, Nat. Commun. 2024), GraphESMStable (ESM + GNN for stability, bioRxiv 2026), SPURS (ESM-2 + ProteinMPNN for stability, Nat. Commun. 2025), Huynh et al. 2025 (dynamics-based GNN for fitness/epistasis, PNAS), ECNet (evolutionary couplings + LSTM for fitness, Nat. Biotechnol. 2020), EGNN-CMutPred (ESM-2 + EGNN for mutation effects, 2025), and EHCube4P (hypercube GCN for fitness, 2025). The authors' claimed differentiator—mutation-centric subgraphs rather than full protein graphs—has some novelty, but this architectural choice is undermined by their own ablations: on the two-protein subset tested with the fitness target, a simple MLP without graph structure outperforms EpiGNN (ρ = 0.603 vs. 0.531), and random edges perform comparably to PLM-derived coupling edges (p = 0.483). Given these negative results, the primary novel insight shifts from architecture to the training objective, which is a valuable but narrower contribution.
strengths: Honest and thorough reporting of negative results: the authors explicitly state that the graph architecture does not outperform an MLP on the tested subset, that PLM coupling edges are not significantly better than random edges, and that the model does not capture epistasis in isolation.

The training objective ablation is striking and well-supported: switching from epistasis residual to direct fitness prediction improves performance by 0.472 Spearman points, a larger effect than any architectural change.
Experimental design is sound: 3-fold cross-validation with 3 seeds, proper statistical testing (Wilcoxon signed-rank), and publicly available data (ProteinGym) and models (ESM-2).
The paper is clearly written, with explicit contributions, well-organized sections, and self-contained figures.
Reproducibility details are good: hyperparameters, compute, seeds, and data preprocessing are all reported.
weaknesses: The core architectural motivation (graph structure + PLM coupling edges improves multi-mutation fitness prediction) is contradicted by the authors' own ablations. This weakens the paper's central claim.
Architecture ablations with the fitness target were run on only 2 of 7 proteins (F7YBW8 and GFP), so general conclusions about the graph architecture's utility are not well-supported.
No cross-protein generalization experiments are reported; evaluation is limited to within-protein cross-validation.
The model does not actually predict epistatic interactions (mean epistasis correlation = -0.111), which is at odds with the paper's title and framing.

The related work could more explicitly discuss how SPIRED-Fitness/Stab and GraphESMStable also combine ESM embeddings with graph attention, albeit on full protein graphs rather than mutation-centric subgraphs.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper's greatest strength is its intellectual honesty, but this honesty also reveals that the main architectural story does not hold up. To improve the paper, I recommend reframing the contribution away from "EpiGNN as a graph architecture" and toward "a systematic study of how to effectively do supervised multi-mutation fitness prediction with PLM features." Specifically: (1) Lead with the training objective finding in the abstract and introduction, as this is the strongest and most generalizable insight. (2) Run the fitness-target MLP ablation on all seven proteins to definitively assess whether the graph structure provides any benefit in general. If the MLP still matches or exceeds EpiGNN on the full set, this should be the headline negative result. (3) Add a discussion of why PLM attention-derived coupling does not outperform random edges for this task—this is an interesting scientific finding that deserves analysis. (4) Consider renaming the model or at least de-emphasizing "epistasis" in the title, since the model does not predict epistasis in isolation. (5) Expand the related work discussion to more directly compare with SPIRED-Fitness/Stab and GraphESMStable, which also use ESM-2 + GAT. (6) If possible, add a cross-protein generalization experiment or discuss it as critical future work.
questions_for_authors: Can you run the fitness-target MLP ablation on all seven proteins? The current conclusion that "the graph architecture does not clearly outperform a simpler MLP" is based on only 2 proteins, which limits its strength.
Why do you think PLM-derived coupling edges do not outperform random edges? Is it because the attention maps already inform the node features, making the edge features redundant?
Given that the model does not capture epistasis in isolation, would you consider reframing the paper around "supervised multi-mutation fitness prediction" rather than "epistasis-aware" modeling?
Did you observe any systematic differences between proteins where EpiGNN beats ridge regression by a large margin (e.g., GFP, SPG1) versus where it loses (HIS7)?
reviews.integrity_check: The results appear genuine. I verified that all numbers in the paper's main table match the protein_results section of results.json. Experiment source code (.py files) is present in exp/ and implements the described method. Training logs in logs/ show actual execution with epoch-level validation Spearman correlations. The code uses the reported seeds (42, 123, 456) and produces 9 fold-seed evaluations per protein. There is a naming quirk in the codebase where the internal key 'epignn' refers to the epistasis-target variant (ρ ≈ 0.157) and 'ablation_fitness_target' refers to the main fitness-target method (ρ ≈ 0.629), but the paper handles this correctly by presenting 'EpiGNN' as the fitness-target version. No evidence of fabricated citations: all key references (Lin et al. 2023, Meier et al. 2021, Nambiar et al. 2025, Huynh et al. 2025, Dieckhaus & Kuhlman 2025, Zhang et al. 2026, Li & Luo 2025, etc.) were verified via Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar, PubMed, or publisher websites.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes EpiGNN, a graph neural network that constructs mutation-centric subgraphs from ESM-2 attention-derived residue coupling to predict multi-mutation protein fitness. Evaluated on seven ProteinGym DMS datasets, EpiGNN (with fitness training objective) achieves mean Spearman ρ=0.629, outperforming additive ESM-2 (0.111) and ridge regression (0.595). The paper's most notable finding is negative: the graph architecture does not outperform a simpler MLP on a two-protein subset, PLM coupling edges are not significantly better than random edges, and the model does not predict epistasis in isolation.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The mutation-centric subgraph construction using PLM attention-derived coupling as edges is a reasonably novel combination. However, several closely related works exist: (1) Huynh et al. 2025 (PNAS) already built a GNN for epistasis/fitness prediction using residue coupling graphs (from MD simulations); EpiGNN replaces MD with PLM attention coupling, but the paper's own results show this coupling is no better than random edges (p=0.483). (2) GraphESMStable (Zhang et al. 2026) combines ESM embeddings with GNNs and an epistasis decoder for stability prediction. (3) Nambiar et al. 2025 already demonstrated that PLM attention maps encode epistatic coupling—EpiGNN operationalizes this but without demonstrating that the coupling actually helps. The name 'EpiGNN' conflicts with an existing published paper (Xie & Zhang, ECML-PKDD 2023) on epidemic forecasting GNNs. The paper's most interesting contribution—that training objectives matter more than architecture—is an empirical observation rather than a methodological advance, and it partially undermines the paper's own architectural proposal.
strengths: Exceptionally honest reporting of negative results: the paper transparently acknowledges that graph structure does not outperform MLP, PLM coupling edges are not better than random, and the model fails to capture epistasis in isolation. This level of intellectual honesty is rare and valuable.

The finding that switching from epistasis-residual to direct fitness prediction improves performance by 0.472 points is a genuinely useful insight for the field, potentially saving other researchers from pursuing the same dead end.
The paper is clearly written with well-organized sections, explicit contribution statements, and self-contained figures and tables.
Evaluation uses 7 diverse DMS datasets from ProteinGym with multiple seeds and folds, providing reasonable coverage of the protein fitness landscape.
All 13 references are real and verifiable publications—no fabricated citations.
weaknesses: Critical fairness issue in the main results table (Table 2): EpiGNN uses the fitness training objective while the MLP baseline uses the epistatic residual objective. The paper acknowledges this in the ablation section, but presenting this as the main comparison is misleading. When both use the fitness target (Table 3), MLP outperforms EpiGNN (0.603 vs 0.531 on the 2-protein subset).

Severe hyperparameter discrepancy between paper and code: The paper reports 300 max epochs, patience 30, batch size 64, and 5-fold CV (matching config.py), but the actual experiment scripts override these to 80 max epochs, patience 10, batch size 128, and 3-fold CV. This means reported hyperparameters do not match the experiments that produced the results.

Architecture ablations (Table 3) are limited to only 2 of 7 proteins (F7YBW8 and GFP), yet these ablations contain the paper's most important findings (MLP > EpiGNN, random edges ≈ PLM edges). The authors acknowledge this limitation but it critically weakens the central claims.

The paper's core architectural contribution—mutation-centric GNN with PLM coupling edges—is undermined by its own results: the graph structure hurts performance vs MLP, and PLM coupling edges provide no advantage over random edges. The paper essentially argues against its own method.

The 'EpiGNN' name is misleading given the model does not predict epistasis (mean epistasis correlation = -0.111), and it conflicts with an existing published method (epidemic forecasting, ECML-PKDD 2023).
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents an interesting research direction but arrives at conclusions that fundamentally undermine its proposed method. The most actionable path forward would be to (1) reframe the paper as a study of what matters for multi-mutation fitness prediction, making the training-objective finding the primary contribution rather than the architecture; (2) run the fitness-target MLP comparison on ALL 7 proteins—this is the single most important missing experiment and would definitively answer whether graph structure helps; (3) fix the hyperparameter reporting to match actual experiments (80 epochs/patience 10/batch 128/3-fold, not 300/30/64/5-fold); (4) present a fair main results table where all supervised methods use the same training objective; (5) rename the method to avoid conflict with the existing EpiGNN publication; and (6) consider whether the paper would be better positioned as a negative-results or insights paper at a venue that values such contributions (e.g., TMLR). The honesty about limitations is commendable and should be preserved, but the current framing—proposing a GNN while simultaneously showing it doesn't work—creates a confused narrative.
questions_for_authors: Why were the actual training hyperparameters (80 epochs, patience 10, batch 128, 3-fold CV) different from those reported in the paper (300 epochs, patience 30, batch 64)? Did you verify that performance converges with these reduced settings?

Why not run the fitness-target MLP comparison on all 7 proteins? This is computationally cheaper than EpiGNN training and would resolve the paper's central open question about whether graph structure helps.

In results.json, the 'epignn' method_summary shows mean Spearman=0.157 (epistasis target), while the paper's main Table 2 reports 0.629 which corresponds to 'ablation_fitness_target'. Can you clarify which was intended as the 'main' method from the start, versus discovered as better through ablation?

Given that the MLP outperforms EpiGNN on both tested proteins with the fitness target, and PLM coupling edges are no better than random, what is the specific scenario where you expect the graph architecture to provide benefit?
The paper claims 'approximately 600K trainable parameters'—can you verify this given the model architecture (1280→128 projection, 2 GATv2 layers with 4x32 heads, MLP readout)?
reviews.integrity_check: Numbers in the paper match results.json consistently across all 7 proteins and all methods (verified per-protein Spearman ρ values and standard deviations). Experiment source code exists and implements the described method (GATv2Conv layers, mutation-centric graph construction, ESM-2 feature extraction). All 13 references verified as real publications. However, there are two significant integrity concerns: (1) The paper reports hyperparameters from config.py (300 epochs, patience 30, batch 64, 5-fold CV) but the actual experiment scripts that produced the results use overridden values (80 epochs, patience 10, batch 128, 3-fold CV)—this is a material discrepancy in reproducibility-critical details. (2) The last two experiment log sessions (claude_1774534909, claude_1774534921) show rate-limit errors with no actual computation performed, suggesting some planned experiments may not have been completed. Earlier logs do contain actual training output with per-fold results.
